content.text: Latency SLA Performance Benchmarks

A latency SLA is not a single number — it is a set of percentile targets (p50, p95, p99) defined per integration path. A streaming chat endpoint has fundamentally different latency characteristics than a batch embedding job or a multi-hop agent chain, so each path needs its own SLO budget.

This sub-subtopic covers how to define per-path SLOs for GenAI workloads, how to capture and store baseline benchmark data, how to detect regressions in CI, and how to structure the benchmark output so a script can evaluate it automatically.

attrs.alt: Latency SLA tiers by GenAI integration path type

content.text: Defining Per-Path Latency Budgets

Start by enumerating every request path in the application and classifying each by whether the user is waiting synchronously. Interactive paths (streaming chat) need aggressive TTFT SLOs; async paths (batch embedding) need throughput SLOs instead.
Time-to-first-token (TTFT): the primary interactive metric. Target p95 < 2s for chat; < 3s is the upper tolerance before users perceive 'lag'.
p95 total latency: covers the full round-trip. For streaming responses this is the time until the stream closes, which grows with response length.
p99 latency: the worst-case budget. If p99 > your client timeout, some users will see errors even at low error rates.
Throughput for batch paths: tokens/second or requests/second per worker. Regression is a throughput drop of > 20% vs baseline.
Capturing Benchmark Results with pytest-benchmark
For unit-level latency benchmarks (e.g. the embedding function alone, the prompt builder, the parser),

marks.type: captures per-call timing and integrates with CI without the overhead of a full Locust run.

content.text:
# tests/perf/test_latency_benchmarks.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from app.embedding import embed_text
from app.prompt import build_rag_prompt
from app.parser import parse_llm_response


def make_fake_embedding(dim=1536):
    return [0.01] * dim


@pytest.fixture
def fake_openai_embed(monkeypatch):
    mock = MagicMock()
    mock.return_value = MagicMock(data=[MagicMock(embedding=make_fake_embedding())])
    monkeypatch.setattr("app.embedding.openai_client.embeddings.create", mock)


def test_embed_text_latency(benchmark, fake_openai_embed):
    """Single embed call must complete in < 50ms (excluding network, mocked here)."""
    result = benchmark(embed_text, "Explain transformer attention mechanisms.")
    assert len(result) == 1536
    # benchmark.stats is populated automatically; pytest-benchmark --max-time gate handles the SLO


def test_build_rag_prompt_latency(benchmark):
    """Prompt construction must complete in < 5ms — it runs on every request."""
    docs = [{"text": "chunk " * 100, "score": 0.9}] * 5
    result = benchmark(build_rag_prompt, query="How does RAG work?", docs=docs)
    assert len(result) > 0


def test_parse_llm_response_latency(benchmark):
    """Parser must handle a 2000-token response in < 2ms."""
    fake_response = "word " * 2000
    result = benchmark(parse_llm_response, fake_response)
    assert result is not None

# Run benchmarks and store results as JSON baseline
pytest tests/perf/ \
  --benchmark-json=results/benchmark_baseline.json \
  --benchmark-min-rounds=20 \
  --benchmark-disable-gc \
  -v

# Compare a new run against the stored baseline (fail if > 10% slower)
pytest tests/perf/ \
  --benchmark-compare=results/benchmark_baseline.json \
  --benchmark-compare-fail=mean:10% \
  --benchmark-json=results/benchmark_pr.json \
  -v
Integration-Level Latency Benchmark Harness

For end-to-end path benchmarks against a running service, write a thin harness that fires a fixed request corpus and records p50/p95/p99 with no dependency on Locust. This is cheaper to run per PR than a full load test.

# tests/perf/benchmark_harness.py
"""
Run with: python tests/perf/benchmark_harness.py --base-url http://staging:8000
Exits non-zero if any SLO threshold is breached.
"""
import argparse, json, os, statistics, sys, time
import httpx

SCENARIOS = [
    {
        "name": "chat_streaming",
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/chat",
        "body": {"message": "Explain retrieval-augmented generation briefly."},
        "stream": True,
        "slo": {"p95_ms": 8000, "ttft_p95_ms": 3000},
    },
    {
        "name": "rag_search",
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/search",
        "body": {"query": "What causes hallucinations in LLMs?"},
        "stream": False,
        "slo": {"p95_ms": 10000},
    },
    {
        "name": "embed_single",
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/embed",
        "body": {"text": "Transformer architecture overview."},
        "stream": False,
        "slo": {"p95_ms": 2000},
    },
]

WARMUP_REQUESTS = 3
BENCHMARK_REQUESTS = 20


def run_scenario(client, scenario):
    latencies_ms = []
    ttft_ms_list = []

    for _ in range(WARMUP_REQUESTS):
        client.request(scenario["method"], scenario["path"], json=scenario["body"])

    for _ in range(BENCHMARK_REQUESTS):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        first_token_t = None

        if scenario.get("stream"):
            with client.stream(scenario["method"], scenario["path"], json=scenario["body"]) as r:
                for chunk in r.iter_bytes():
                    if chunk and first_token_t is None:
                        first_token_t = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        else:
            client.request(scenario["method"], scenario["path"], json=scenario["body"])

        elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        latencies_ms.append(elapsed)
        if first_token_t is not None:
            ttft_ms_list.append(first_token_t)

    sorted_l = sorted(latencies_ms)
    n = len(sorted_l)
    result = {
        "name": scenario["name"],
        "p50_ms": sorted_l[n // 2],
        "p95_ms": sorted_l[int(n * 0.95)],
        "p99_ms": sorted_l[int(n * 0.99)],
        "mean_ms": statistics.mean(latencies_ms),
    }
    if ttft_ms_list:
        result["ttft_p95_ms"] = sorted(ttft_ms_list)[int(len(ttft_ms_list) * 0.95)]
    return result


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--base-url", default="http://localhost:8000")
    parser.add_argument("--output", default="results/benchmark_results.json")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = httpx.Client(base_url=args["base_url"], timeout=60)
    results = []
    failures = []

    for scenario in SCENARIOS:
        print(f"  Benchmarking {scenario['name']} ...")
        result = run_scenario(client, scenario)
        results.append(result)

        # Evaluate SLOs
        for metric, threshold in scenario["slo"].items():
            if result.get(metric, 0) > threshold:
                failures.append(
                    f"{scenario['name']}.{metric} = {result[metric]:.0f}ms > {threshold}ms SLO"
                )

    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(args["output"]), exist_ok=True)
    with open(args["output"], "w") as f:
        json.dump({"results": results, "failures": failures}, f, indent=2)

    if failures:
        print("SLO FAILURES:")
        for f in failures:
            print(f"  ✗ {f}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print(f"All SLOs passed. Results: {args['output']}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Storing and Comparing Baselines Across Releases

A single benchmark run is only useful if you can compare it to a known good state. Store the baseline JSON in the repository (or an artifact store) tagged to the release SHA, and fail CI when regressions exceed the threshold.
Common SLO Anti-Patterns for GenAI Paths
Anti-Pattern
Problem
Correct Approach
Single p50 SLO only
Misses the long tail; p99 timeouts affect real users
Set p50 / p95 / p99 targets for every interactive path
Same SLO for all paths
Chat and batch embedding have 10× different latency profiles
Define separate SLO tiers per path class (T1–T4)
Measuring total RTT for streaming
RTT grows linearly with response length; TTFT stays stable
Gate on TTFT p95, not total RTT, for streaming paths
Running benchmark against prod LLM
Adds token cost and external variability to CI runs
Use a mocked LLM stub for micro-benchmarks; real LLM only in nightly runs
Updating baselines automatically on every merge
Regressions silently become the new normal
Only update baseline after an explicit release tag or manual approval
CI Workflow for Nightly Benchmark Runs
Micro-benchmarks (

marks.type: ) can run on every PR in seconds because they mock the LLM. Integration benchmarks run nightly against staging with a real (or lightly loaded) LLM, then compare against the pinned baseline artifact.

content.text:
# .github/workflows/benchmarks.yml
name: Performance Benchmarks

on:
  push:
    branches: ["main"]        # micro-benchmarks on every merge to main
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 3 * * *"      # full integration benchmark nightly
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  micro-benchmarks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt pytest-benchmark

      - name: Run micro-benchmarks
        run: |
          pytest tests/perf/ \
            --benchmark-json=results/benchmark_pr.json \
            --benchmark-min-rounds=20 \
            --benchmark-disable-gc \
            -v

      - name: Compare against baseline
        run: |
          python scripts/compare_benchmarks.py \
            benchmarks/baseline.json \
            results/benchmark_pr.json

      - name: Upload results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: micro-benchmarks-${{ github.sha }}
          path: results/

  integration-benchmarks:
    if: github.event_name == 'schedule' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt httpx

      - name: Run integration benchmark harness
        env:
          STAGING_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_URL }}
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python tests/perf/benchmark_harness.py \
            --base-url "$STAGING_URL" \
            --output results/integration_results.json

      - name: Compare against integration baseline
        run: |
          python scripts/compare_benchmarks.py \
            benchmarks/integration_baseline.json \
            results/integration_results.json

      - name: Upload results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: integration-benchmarks-${{ github.run_id }}
          path: results/
          retention-days: 60
Updating the Baseline After a Release

Baselines should only be updated deliberately — not automatically on every merge — to prevent regressions from silently becoming the new normal. The update workflow is triggered manually after a release is tagged and verified.

# scripts/update_baseline.py
# Run manually: python scripts/update_baseline.py results/benchmark_pr.json
import json, sys, shutil, subprocess
from pathlib import Path

src = Path(sys.argv[1])
baseline_path = Path("benchmarks/baseline.json")

if not src.exists():
    print(f"ERROR: {src} does not exist")
    sys.exit(1)

# Validate the new file has results
with open(src) as f:
    data = json.load(f)

if not data.get("results"):
    print("ERROR: source file has no 'results' key — aborting baseline update")
    sys.exit(1)

failures = data.get("failures", [])
if failures:
    print(f"ERROR: source run had {len(failures)} SLO failure(s) — baseline must be a clean run")
    for f in failures:
        print(f"  {f}")
    sys.exit(1)

# Archive the old baseline
old_sha = subprocess.check_output(["git", "rev-parse", "HEAD"]).decode().strip()[:8]
archive = baseline_path.parent / f"baseline_{old_sha}.json"
shutil.copy(baseline_path, archive)
print(f"Archived old baseline to {archive}")

# Install new baseline
shutil.copy(src, baseline_path)
print(f"Updated baseline: {baseline_path}")
print(f"New p95 values:")
for r in data["results"]:
    print(f"  {r['name']}: p95={r.get('p95_ms', 'N/A'):.0f}ms")
Alerting on Production Latency Regressions

CI benchmarks guard against pre-deploy regressions. Production monitoring closes the loop by alerting when real user latency drifts above the SLO budget — catches regressions from LLM provider changes, traffic mix shifts, and resource exhaustion.

# app/middleware/latency_tracker.py
import time, statistics
from collections import defaultdict, deque
from fastapi import Request
import structlog

log = structlog.get_logger()

# Rolling window: last 5 minutes of samples per path
_samples: dict[str, deque] = defaultdict(lambda: deque(maxlen=500))

SLO_THRESHOLDS_MS = {
    "/chat": {"p95": 8000, "ttft_p95": 3000},
    "/search": {"p95": 10000},
    "/embed": {"p95": 2000},
}


async def latency_tracking_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000

    path = request.url.path
    _samples[path].append(elapsed_ms)

    # Check SLO every 100 requests per path
    if len(_samples[path]) % 100 == 0:
        slo = SLO_THRESHOLDS_MS.get(path)
        if slo:
            sorted_s = sorted(_samples[path])
            n = len(sorted_s)
            p95 = sorted_s[int(n * 0.95)]
            if p95 > slo.get("p95", float("inf")):
                log.warning(
                    "latency_slo_breach",
                    path=path,
                    p95_ms=round(p95),
                    slo_ms=slo["p95"],
                    sample_count=n,
                )

    return response
Summary

Latency SLAs for GenAI applications must be defined per path and per percentile. Interactive streaming paths need a TTFT SLO of p95 < 2–3s; RAG search paths need a p95 total latency SLO of < 8–10s; batch and agent paths have wider budgets because they are not synchronous user-facing calls.
Store a benchmark baseline JSON at every release, compare it in CI using a regression threshold (20% slower triggers a failure), and separate the micro-benchmark suite (

marks.type: with mocked LLM) from the integration harness (real service against staging) so the fast path runs on every PR and the expensive path runs nightly.
